Design Critiques That Actually Improve Work
A practical structure for critique sessions that sharpen decisions, not egos.

Critique quality is mostly a facilitation problem.

When sessions anchor on goals, evidence, and constraints, feedback becomes actionable instead of subjective. I use a lightweight format that separates observation, interpretation, and recommendation.

Teams leave with clearer decisions and faster iteration cycles.

When to Standardize and When to Bend the Rules
Consistency matters, but rigid systems can become friction points if misapplied.

Design systems scale quality, but they should not block meaningful problem-solving.

Standards work best when teams understand intent, not just component availability. For edge cases, intentional exceptions can preserve clarity and improve outcomes.

A healthy system balances guardrails with room for context.

Why Good UX Starts With Better Questions
Framing assumptions early helps teams avoid expensive design detours later.

Strong UX outcomes are often decided before pixels are explored.

When teams align on problem framing, constraints, and success criteria, design work moves faster with less churn.

Question quality is a leverage point: better questions produce better options.

Simple moderation shifts can dramatically improve research signal quality.

Leading prompts create false confidence and weak product decisions.

I prefer neutral language, concrete follow-ups, and task reconstruction over opinion polling. This reveals real behavior and tradeoffs instead of aspirational answers.

A small interview guide rewrite often improves signal more than recruiting more participants.

Redesigned browsing and filtering model to make learning paths more discoverable.

Learners struggled to find relevant courses beyond homepage recommendations.

I reworked taxonomy and filtering patterns, introduced stronger metadata cues, and improved browse-to-enroll continuity across devices.

Post-launch data showed better discovery depth and higher click-through into course detail pages.

Introduced progressive disclosure that cut support tickets tied to transfer errors.

This project focused on reducing user mistakes in a high-risk transaction flow.

I redesigned form structure around user intent, improved confirmations, and clarified fee/timing language at key decision points. We tested multiple prototypes and iterated quickly with compliance stakeholders.

The shipped flow lowered transfer-related support requests while improving completion confidence.

Mapped end-to-end service touchpoints to improve completion and reduce administrative overhead.

The intake journey spanned digital forms, staff handoffs, and in-person checkpoints.

I mapped service touchpoints to find friction between systems and teams, then proposed an intake model with clearer status visibility and fewer redundant steps.

The result was improved form completion rates and less manual rework for front-desk staff.

Reframed analytics hierarchy, reduced cognitive load, and increased daily active usage.

I partnered with product and engineering leads to simplify a dense analytics surface into a clear decision-making workflow.

The redesign prioritized actionable metrics, progressive disclosure, and better grouping of related tasks. We validated changes through task-based usability sessions and phased releases.

Outcomes included stronger weekly engagement and fewer support tickets tied to dashboard interpretation.

Prototyped interface cues that communicate confidence in recommendations without overwhelming users.

This concept focused on trust calibration in recommendation-heavy interfaces.

I tested lightweight confidence indicators, explanatory hover states, and reversible actions to reduce the fear of committing too early.

The resulting pattern set provided a clearer language for uncertainty while preserving a clean interaction flow.

Tested whether adaptive prompt sequencing could reduce onboarding friction in high-choice workflows.

This experiment explored how onboarding can change as users reveal intent signals.

Instead of one static walkthrough, the prototype adapted prompts based on initial actions and skipped irrelevant guidance.

The output was a rules framework for adaptive onboarding logic that can be reused across products with different complexity profiles.
